cataloging_subject.label: The Works of John Metham (Craig/U-M encoded collection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-level collection represented by the pinned U-M/CME XML and delimited by Hardin Craig's 1916 EETS volume: Amoryus and Cleopes and four prose work families, with Palmistry represented from two witnesses and the Christmas-Day prognostication represented by a complete copy and a fragment. The collection contains five medieval abstract works but seven encoded textual representations. Craig's editorial front matter is retained as paratext with an item count of zero; the unencoded glossary and index are not medieval works. Manuscript witnesses, printed editions, facsimiles, and digital encodings are transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

catalog_summary.title: The Works of John Metham (Craig/U-M encoded collection)
catalog_summary.author: John Metham: author, translator, or compiler by work; the two Christmas-Day texts are attributed to him
catalog_summary.editor: Hardin Craig (1916 source edition); University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative (1999 digital encoding)
catalog_summary.date: Amoryus and Cleopes composed in the twenty-seventh regnal year of Henry VI, 1 September 1448-31 August 1449; other work dates unresolved; collection first published 1916
catalog_summary.region: East Anglia, especially Norfolk; more specific places of composition are uncertain

titles.value: The Works of John Metham (Craig/U-M encoded collection)
notes: The qualifier makes the edition-level boundary visible and avoids treating the composite volume as one medieval work or a complete authorial corpus.
Craig's edition title groups two full witness versions under this work label.

agents.name: John Metham
notes: A fifteenth-century East Anglian writer; work-specific responsibility is recorded below rather than projected uniformly over the collection.

scope.description: The Palmistry work represented by both Garrett and All Souls copies

notes: The text says Metham translated it in his twenty-fifth winter; this biographical age is not normalized into a calendar year.
Craig identifies Latin physiognomical source traditions, but no exact exemplar has been established.

scope.description: The complete and fragmentary Christmas-Day versions in Garrett 141

notes: The two texts are not explicitly signed in the way that Metham's neighboring prose works are; attribution is retained as attributed rather than known.

scope.description: The 1916 collection, its transcription, supplied organization, notes, and front matter
Parallel Garrett and All Souls versions

date_statements.display: Twenty-seventh regnal year of Henry VI (1 September 1448-31 August 1449)
notes: The poem's regnal-year statement is more precise than a simple display date of 1449.

date_statements.display: Mid-fifteenth century; exact work dates unresolved
notes: The Palmistry's reference to Metham's twenty-fifth winter does not supply a secure absolute date.

places.label: East Anglia
notes: Broad literary and dialect region; exact composition locations differ in certainty by work.

places.label: Norfolk
notes: The Garrett manuscript and Metham's language are associated with Norfolk; this does not prove one copying or composition site for every item.

scope.description: Latin citations, source names, and formulae in medieval texts and editorial matter

form_statements.description: The encoded collection combines one verse romance, six prose witness representations, and editorial paratext containing prose and quoted verse.

summaries.value: The encoded collection joins John Metham's 2,211-line Amoryus and Cleopes with prose works on palmistry, physiognomy, Christmas-Day prognostication, and lunar-day prognostication. Palmistry appears in full parallel versions from Princeton Garrett MS 141 and All Souls College MS 81; the Christmas-Day work appears as a complete Garrett copy and a separate Garrett flyleaf fragment.

The five abstract medieval works, seven encoded witness representations, Craig's editorial paratext, two manuscript codices, and later edition and encoding layers are intentionally not flattened into one object.

extent.description: Intellectual and representation metrics for the medieval content: five abstract works and seven encoded textual representations because Palmistry has two full witness versions and the Christmas-Day prognostication has a complete version and a fragment. Amoryus and Cleopes has 2,211 numbered intellectual lines in 319 stanza groups. The XML has 2,248 L nodes overall: 2,217 under the poem (including two editorial-note quotation lines), twenty-eight in the introduction, and three in the fragment note. Editorial paratext has item_count 0 and the unencoded glossary and index are excluded.

content_parts.label: Craig editorial paratext
content_parts.summary: Encoded front matter: preface, contents, eight-section introduction, and the printed title opening Amoryus and Cleopes. It is publication-layer material, not a medieval work.
notes: Craig's glossary (pp. 167-177) and index (pp. 178-184) are not encoded; their absence does not create additional medieval-work parts.

content_parts.label: Amoryus and Cleopes
content_parts.summary: A four-book Middle English verse romance adapting the Pyramus and Thisbe story and surviving uniquely in Garrett MS 141.
notes: Craig reports two cancelled, illegible stanzas after the ending. A current METS web edition reports recovering erased material through modern imaging; that later intervention is not projected into the pinned XML or Craig's 1916 text.

content_parts.label: First Book
content_parts.summary: The seventy-line prologue and first narrative book; U-M notes that the following division is conjectural and follows Craig.

content_parts.label: Second Book
content_parts.summary: The second top-level book division in Craig's edition.

content_parts.label: Third Book
content_parts.summary: The third top-level book division, with its seven-line prologue.
notes: The XML has 757 L nodes here because two printed lines are split into duplicate-numbered nodes.

content_parts.label: Last Book
content_parts.summary: The fourth and final book, including its twenty-eight-line prologue.
notes: The XML subtree has 437 L nodes: 435 numbered nodes plus two quotation lines nested inside Craig's note at line 2193. Several printed lines are represented by split duplicate-numbered nodes.

content_parts.label: Palmistry
content_parts.summary: Metham's Middle English translation of a Latin chiromancy text attributed in the tradition to Doctor Aurelian, printed in two full parallel witness versions.

content_parts.label: Palmistry: Garrett version
content_parts.summary: The fuller version from Garrett MS 141, used by Craig as the principal parallel text.

content_parts.label: Palmistry: All Souls version
content_parts.summary: A closely related full version from All Souls College MS 81, generally later in spelling and omitting two short paragraphs found in Garrett.

locators.scheme: historical folios used by Craig and Coxe

content_parts.label: Physiognomy
content_parts.summary: Metham's compilation and translation from Latin physiognomical traditions associated with Loxus, Polemon, Aristotle, and the Secretum secretorum.

content_parts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostications
content_parts.summary: Two separate Garrett representations of the Esdras-associated Christmas-Day prognostication tradition: one complete copy and one damaged flyleaf fragment.

content_parts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostication: complete Garrett version
content_parts.summary: The complete version in the main Garrett codex sequence.

content_parts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostication: Garrett flyleaf fragment
content_parts.summary: A second version on a spoiled sheet reused as a flyleaf; the text breaks off on the third line of its second page.
notes: Three XML L nodes belong to Craig's editorial note about the fragment, not to a verse work.

content_parts.label: The Days of the Moon
content_parts.summary: Metham's prose compilation of prognostications for lunar days in the lunationes tradition.

related_works.title: Ovidian Pyramus and Thisbe tradition
notes: Relation is at the narrative-work level; no physical Ovid exemplar used by Metham is identified.

related_works.title: Latin chiromancy attributed to Doctor Aurelian
notes: The exact Latin work and exemplar remain unidentified.

related_works.title: Latin physiognomical tradition of Loxus, Polemon, and Aristotle
Secretum secretorum, physiognomical section
notes: Craig associates material with the Latin translation by Philip of Tripoli; an exact exemplar is not known.

evidence.summary: Primary repository artifact; supplies the exact encoded boundary, titles, source statement, editorial declaration, page and folio milestones, and text.

evidence.citation: Computational structural and line-number inspection of the pinned Metham XML, checked against Craig's 1916 edition.
evidence.summary: Established five abstract works, seven medieval representations, front-matter and omitted-back-matter boundaries, division paths, 319 verse groups, canonical 2,211-line extent, and raw XML node anomalies.

evidence.citation: John Metham, The Works of John Metham, ed. Hardin Craig, EETS OS 132 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1916).
evidence.summary: Immediate textual ancestor of the 1974 reprint and primary evidence for edition boundary, work titles, authorship, source analysis, parallel Palmistry presentation, manuscript loci, and poem extent.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, Metham.
evidence.summary: Current item landing, table of contents, persistent identifier, and provider rights statement for the electronic edition.

evidence.citation: MEDictionary, current public Metham XML, last updated May 2026.
evidence.summary: Current descendant encoding consulted for comparison with the pinned artifact; core divisions and counts persist, while markup is modernized and two substantive corrected-reading differences were identified.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library, Middle English Works, Garrett MS 141.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark, contents, codicological description, date and place, language, and complete 180-image digital object for the principal manuscript witness.

evidence.citation: All Souls College Library, Catalogue of Manuscripts, MS 81, online edition (2022).
evidence.summary: Official codex description and historical foliation showing Palmistry beginning at old fol. 202 and ending before the next item at old fol. 213b.

evidence.citation: Andrew G. Watson, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Medieval Manuscripts of All Souls College, Oxford (1997), MS 81.
evidence.summary: Current foliation, codicological strata, hand and language description, and the exact crosswalk of Metham's Palmistry to fols. 175v-185v.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, current Amoryus and Cleopes edition and introduction.
evidence.summary: Current scholarship for the poem's date, East Anglian context, unique Garrett witness, Ovidian adaptation, literary genres, and recovery of erased ending material in the current web edition.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin chiromancy work and exemplar underlie Metham's Palmistry translation attributed to Doctor Aurelian?
next_steps: Compare the text against cataloged Latin Aurelian chiromancies and recent work on Middle English palmistry before assigning a normalized source-work identity.

open_questions.question: Can Metham's exact Latin exemplars for the Physiognomy compilation be identified beyond Craig's broad Loxus-Polemon-Aristotle and Secretum secretorum source families?
next_steps: Consult recent source studies and compare distinctive chapter sequences against Latin witnesses.

notes: The All Souls folio systems are both retained: Craig and the older Coxe description use 202r-212v, while Watson's current foliation locates the same text at 175v-185v.

Raw XML L-node counts are encoding metrics, not authored line counts. Duplicate, split, truncated, and missing @N values prevent a naive node or number count from replacing Craig's canonical 2,211-line statement.
The U-M encoding integrates Craig's printed notes from pp. 159-166 into the text and omits those print pages as a separate section; it also omits the glossary and index.
The first/second book division after line 723 is conjectural in the electronic edition and follows Craig's organization.
Current MEDictionary XML is a descendant public encoding, not an upstream source of the pinned repository file.
